subject: Physics
unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: A living room has dimensions \(5.6 \, \text{m} \times 3.6 \, \text{m} \times 2.4 \, \text{m}\). Air density is \(1.29 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3\). What is the approximate mass of air in the room?
questions.hint: Volume = length × width × height. Then mass = density × volume.
questions.explanation: Volume \(V = 5.6 \times 3.6 \times 2.4 = 48.384 \, \text{m}^3\). Mass \(m = \rho V = 1.29 \times 48.384 = 62.4 \, \text{kg} \approx 62 \, \text{kg}\). Option C results from multiplying by 1000 (wrong density conversion).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
source_trace.content_anchor: mass of air in a living room

explanations_tiered.definition: Mass is the quantity of matter, calculated as density multiplied by volume.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Room air mass is found from room volume and air density. Volume is product of three dimensions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Mass of air in a room is like filling the whole room with air and weighing it. Bigger room = more mass.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: First compute room volume \(V = lwh\). Then \(m = \rho V\) with \(\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.29 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Air density varies with temperature and pressure; at STP (0°C, 1 atm), \(\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.29 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3\). Mass \(m = \rho V\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write volume formula: \(V = l \times w \times h\).
reasoning_steps: Calculate room volume from given dimensions.
misconceptions: Confusing volume with area leads to missing one dimension.
Using density in g/cm³ without converting to kg/m³ gives wrong mass.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Room air mass = density × (l×w×h). Air density ≈ 1.29 kg/m³.

glossary_entry.definition: Quantity of matter in an object, measured in kilograms.

flashcard.front: How to find mass of air in a room?
flashcard.back: Multiply air density (1.29 kg/m³) by room volume (l×w×h).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Room 5.6×3.6×2.4 m, air density 1.29 kg/m³. Find air mass.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Mass per unit volume; for air at STP, 1.29 kg/m³.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: m = ρV for air in room.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to multiply all three dimensions for volume.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine the density of an object

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: What is defined as the mass per unit volume of a material?
questions.hint: It measures how tightly matter is packed.
questions.explanation: Density \(\rho = m/V\) is the mass per unit volume. Specific gravity is a ratio of densities, not mass per volume directly.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
source_trace.content_anchor: density of a material is defined as the mass per unit volume

explanations_tiered.definition: Density is the mass of a substance divided by its volume, \(\rho = m/V\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Density is an intensive property; it does not change with sample size and identifies pure substances.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Density tells how much stuff (mass) is in a given space (volume). Heavy for size = high density.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Density \(\rho = m/V\) has SI units kg/m³. For water, \(\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Density is a state function: \(\rho(T,P) = \frac{m}{V(T,P)}\). For gases, \(\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}\).
reasoning_steps: Match "mass per unit volume" to term "density".
misconceptions: Density is sometimes confused with mass; density is mass per volume, not total mass.
Some think density changes with sample size; it does not for pure substances.

glossary_entry.definition: Mass per unit volume of a substance.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Mass per unit volume of a material.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare density of different materials using table.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: What is the term for the force per unit area acting on an object that causes deformation?
questions.hint: It measures the intensity of internal forces.
questions.explanation: Stress \(\sigma = F/A\) is the force per unit area causing deformation. Strain is the fractional change in length, not force per area.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
source_trace.content_anchor: force per unit area acting on an object is the stress

explanations_tiered.definition: Stress is force per unit area, \(\sigma = F/A\), measured in N/m².
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Stress describes the internal force distribution within a deformed object.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Stress is how hard you push or pull on a certain area. More force or smaller area = more stress.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Tensile stress stretches; compressive stress squashes. Both \(\sigma = F_{\perp}/A\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Stress tensor has normal and shear components. Normal stress \(\sigma_n\) acts perpendicular to area.
reasoning_steps: Identify that stress is force per area.
misconceptions: Stress and pressure are not identical; pressure is stress in fluids at rest.
Some think stress has same units as force; stress = force/area (N/m²).

glossary_entry.definition: Force per unit area causing deformation.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Stress = force per unit area, \(\sigma = F/A\), unit N/m².
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Force per unit area causing deformation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize stress is internal force intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: Under the elastic limit, how is stress related to strain?

options.C: Equal to the square root of strain

questions.explanation: Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain according to Hooke's law: \(\sigma = Y \epsilon\). Option A would imply decreasing stress with increasing strain, which is incorrect.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
source_trace.content_anchor: stress is directly proportional to strain

explanations_tiered.definition: Hooke's law states stress is proportional to strain within elastic limit.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Linear elasticity: \(\sigma = Y \epsilon\), where Y is Young's modulus.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like stretching a spring: more pull (stress) = more stretch (strain). They increase together.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Within elastic limit, stress and strain have constant ratio Y: \(\sigma = Y \epsilon\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For small deformations, the stress-strain relationship is linear due to atomic bond stretching.
reasoning_steps: Constant of proportionality is Young's modulus.
misconceptions: Some think stress/strain relationship is always linear; it is only linear within elastic limit.
Inverse proportionality would mean more stress = less strain, which is false.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Within elastic limit: stress ∝ strain (Hooke's law).

glossary_entry.definition: Stress is directly proportional to strain within elastic limit.

flashcard.front: How are stress and strain related under elastic limit?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Linear relationship between stress and strain within elastic limit.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use spring analogy for direct proportionality.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Stress is directly proportional to strain under elastic limit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: What is the condition of an object that remains at rest with all opposing forces balanced?
questions.explanation: Static equilibrium occurs when an object is at rest and all forces and torques acting on it are balanced (net force = 0, net torque = 0). Option B involves constant velocity motion.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
source_trace.content_anchor: system is in static equilibrium if it is at rest and all forces and other factors influencing the object are balanced

explanations_tiered.definition: Static equilibrium is a state where an object is at rest with zero net force and zero net torque.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Both translational and rotational equilibrium must hold for static equilibrium.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A book sitting on a table is in static equilibrium—it's not moving and forces balance.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Static equilibrium requires \(\sum \vec{F} = 0\) and \(\sum \vec{\tau} = 0\). The object is at rest.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In static equilibrium, all particles have zero velocity and acceleration. Conditions: \(\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = 0\), \(\vec{\tau}_{\text{net}} = 0\).
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: object at rest with balanced forces.
Dynamic equilibrium involves constant motion, not rest.
misconceptions: Static equilibrium is sometimes confused with dynamic equilibrium; static = at rest.
Some think only forces need to balance; torques must also balance.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Static equilibrium: at rest, net force = 0, net torque = 0.

glossary_entry.definition: State where an object is at rest with zero net force and zero net torque.

flashcard.back: Object at rest with balanced forces and torques.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Object at rest with net force = 0 and net torque = 0.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: State of rest with balanced forces and torques.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Static equilibrium = at rest with balanced forces.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: A book rests on a table. What happens to the net force on the book if the table exerts an upward normal force exactly equal to the book's weight?
questions.explanation: The first condition of equilibrium requires net force = 0. Weight downward equals normal force upward, so they cancel giving zero net force. Option D ignores the normal force completely.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
source_trace.content_anchor: sum of all forces acting on it is equal to zero

explanations_tiered.definition: The first condition of equilibrium states that the vector sum of all forces acting on an object is zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For translational equilibrium, forces in each direction must balance: \(\sum F_x = 0\), \(\sum F_y = 0\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you push a box from left and right with equal force, it doesn't move—net force is zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Weight \(mg\) downward equals normal force \(N\) upward, so \(\sum F_y = N - mg = 0\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: First condition derives from Newton's second law: \(\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = m\vec{a}\). For \(\vec{a} = 0\), \(\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = 0\).
reasoning_steps: Net force = 0 satisfies first equilibrium condition.
misconceptions: Some think net force zero means no forces act; forces can act as long as they cancel.
Forgetting to include normal force is common.

glossary_entry.definition: Net force on an object must be zero for translational equilibrium.

flashcard.front: What is the first condition of equilibrium?

visual_system.visual_title: Free-Body Diagram of a Book on a Table
visual_system.visual_description: A box labeled 'book' with downward arrow labeled 'weight (mg)' and upward arrow labeled 'normal force (N)' of equal length.
visual_system.caption: Figure 1: Forces on a book in static equilibrium.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Free-body diagram: book with equal upward normal force and downward weight, net force zero.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Net force on book at rest on table?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Zero, because weight = normal force (first condition).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use free-body diagrams to visualize force balance.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Net force = 0 because N = mg.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: What quantity must be zero for an object to be in rotational equilibrium?
questions.explanation: The second condition of equilibrium requires net torque \(\sum \vec{\tau} = 0\), meaning no rotational acceleration. Net force zero (first condition) handles translation, not rotation.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
source_trace.content_anchor: net torque applied to it should also be equal to zero

explanations_tiered.definition: Rotational equilibrium requires zero net torque: \(\sum \vec{\tau} = 0\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Torque causes angular acceleration. Zero net torque means no rotation or constant angular velocity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A seesaw balances when clockwise torque equals counterclockwise torque—net torque is zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Second condition: \(\sum \tau = 0\). Clockwise torques equal counterclockwise torques.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Torque \(\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}\). For static equilibrium, \(\sum \vec{\tau} = 0\) about any axis.
reasoning_steps: For no rotation, net torque must be zero.
This is the second condition of equilibrium.
misconceptions: Some think net force zero is enough; rotation also requires net torque zero.
Torque is sometimes confused with force; torque = force × lever arm.

glossary_entry.definition: Net torque on an object must be zero for rotational equilibrium.

flashcard.front: What is the second condition of equilibrium?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What must be zero for rotational equilibrium?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Zero net torque for rotational equilibrium.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State first and second conditions of equilibrium
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Second condition requires net torque = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: A 30 kg child sits 2.5 m from the pivot of a seesaw. Where must a 25 kg child sit to balance the seesaw?
questions.hint: Use torque balance: \(m_1 g r_1 = m_2 g r_2\).
questions.explanation: Torque balance: \(m_A g r_A = m_B g r_B\). So \(30 \times 2.5 = 25 \times r_B\), thus \(r_B = (30 \times 2.5) / 25 = 3.0 \, \text{m}\). Option A would give equal torque only if masses equal.

calculation_steps: Set clockwise torque = counterclockwise torque: \(m_A g r_A = m_B g r_B\)

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body

explanations_tiered.definition: Torque is force times lever arm: \(\tau = F r\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For rotational equilibrium, clockwise torque equals counterclockwise torque.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heavier child must sit closer to pivot; lighter child sits farther to balance.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Balance condition: \(m_1 r_1 = m_2 r_2\). Solve for unknown distance.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Sum of torques about pivot: \(m_A g r_A - m_B g r_B = 0\) → \(m_A r_A = m_B r_B\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write torque balance: \(m_A g r_A = m_B g r_B\).
Step 2: Cancel g: \(m_A r_A = m_B r_B\).
misconceptions: Assuming equal distances for different masses is common.
Forgetting that torque depends on both force and distance.

glossary_entry.definition: Rotational effect of a force; \(\tau = Fr\).

flashcard.front: 30 kg at 2.5 m, 25 kg at ? m to balance seesaw?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Balance seesaw: 30 kg at 2.5 m, 25 kg at ?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Force times lever arm, causing rotation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that g cancels in torque balance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: In Figure 3.13, a horizontal cord has a force of 30 N. The other cord makes a 30° angle with the horizontal. What is the tension F in the angled cord?
questions.explanation: Horizontal equilibrium: \(30 - F \cos 30° = 0\). So \(F = 30 / \cos 30° = 30 / 0.866 = 34.9 \, \text{N}\). Option C is the vertical component \(F \sin 30°\).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body

explanations_tiered.definition: First condition of equilibrium: \(\sum F_x = 0\), \(\sum F_y = 0\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Forces are resolved into components; horizontal components must cancel.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pull sideways with 30 N; angled rope must pull horizontally with 30 N too to not move sideways.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Force vector equilibrium: \(\vec{F}_{\text{horizontal}} + \vec{F}_{\text{angled}} = 0\). Component method gives \(F_x = F \cos \theta\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify horizontal forces: 30 N to right, \(F \cos 30°\) to left.
reasoning_steps: Resolve angled force into horizontal component.
Set sum of horizontal forces to zero.
misconceptions: Using sin instead of cos for horizontal component.
Forgetting to resolve forces into components.

glossary_entry.definition: Projection of a force vector onto an axis.

flashcard.front: 30 N horizontal, angled rope at 30°, find tension?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find tension F if horizontal force 30 N, angle 30°.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Part of a force along a direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body
questions.question: A 30 kg child sits 2.5 m from the pivot on a seesaw. A 25 kg child sits on the opposite side. How far from the pivot must the 25 kg child sit to balance?
questions.explanation: Balance condition: \(30 \times 2.5 = 25 \times r_2\). Thus \(r_2 = (30 \times 2.5) / 25 = 3.0 \, \text{m}\). Option A would be correct if masses were reversed.

calculation_steps: Torque balance: \(m_1 g r_1 = m_2 g r_2\)

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Elasticity and Static Equilibrium of Rigid Body

explanations_tiered.definition: Torque balance for rotational equilibrium: clockwise torque = counterclockwise torque.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For seesaw balance, product of mass and distance from pivot must be equal on both sides.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heavier child sits closer to center; lighter child sits farther. 30 kg at 2.5 m balances 25 kg at 3.0 m.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Sum of torques about pivot: \(\sum \tau = m_1 g r_1 - m_2 g r_2 = 0\) → \(m_1 r_1 = m_2 r_2\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write torque balance equation: \(m_1 r_1 = m_2 r_2\).
reasoning_steps: Set clockwise torque equal to counterclockwise torque.
misconceptions: Assuming both children sit at same distance regardless of mass.
Forgetting to cancel g leads to same result but extra step.

glossary_entry.definition: Rotational equilibrium condition for a lever.

flashcard.front: 30 kg at 2.5 m, 25 kg at ? m to balance seesaw?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Balance seesaw: 30 kg at 2.5 m, 25 kg at ?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Condition where net torque is zero.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use real seesaw demo if possible.
